quote: We built a solar design for your home. And on my end, it says we actually never had the chance to show it to you or get you the details.
stewart_read: Strategic read holds. On a recycled callback, the design-framing opener is the correct move — it repositions the appointment as completing something already in progress rather than initiating a new sales pitch. This is the moment that converts the customer's 'From where?' challenge into a scheduling conversation. The rep is not over-explaining solar; she's using the existing design as a legitimacy anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: rep is skipping qualification entirely and jumping to booking, which could produce a no-show if the customer has no real memory of or investment in the prior contact.
coaching_implication: This opener is worth preserving as a recycled-lead template. Manager should note it as a positive example of scope-disciplined framing — the rep didn't pitch, she scheduled. The risk is that the customer's 'my mom must have been there' response at 01:45 suggests he may have low personal investment in the prior design. The opener worked here; the closer needs to re-establish that investment.

quote: I I I don't I wasn't there for that appointment. My mom must have been there. I'm not a 100% sure.
stewart_read: This is a co-decision-maker disclosure, not a spouse-handling moment. The customer reveals that his mother was the prior contact — she may be on the title, may be the primary decision-maker, and is not confirmed for the Friday appointment. Meg acknowledges the uncertainty ('Gotcha') and moves to bill logistics without addressing whether the mother needs to be present. The codex is explicit: co-decision-makers must be included in the appointment. Secondary reference: protocols.spouse_decision applies by analogy.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg may have assessed that William is the primary contact (he returned the call, he's scheduling) and that the mother's prior presence was incidental. Without knowing Ion's CRM record, she can't confirm the mother's role.
coaching_implication: Manager should walk the rep through protocols.adult_child_decision_maker. The correct move at 01:45 was a single clarifying question: 'Is your mom on the account with you, or is this your call to make?' If she's on the title, she needs to be on the appointment. The closer is walking into this blind.

quote: From ION Solar. Are you returning a missed call?
stewart_read: On a self-initiated callback, the legitimacy-establishment burden is materially lower than on a cold outbound call. The customer called ION back — he already accepted the premise of the contact. Meg's response is functional but bare: no Tesla framing, no state mention, no design reference until 00:22. The codex defines intro variants for tesla and standard leads but has no recycled-lead intro variant. This is a codex gap, not a standard-intro violation. The callback context makes the abbreviated intro defensible; the absence of a defined recycled-lead intro template is the real flag.
alternative_hypothesis: Primary read (now elevated): the callback context and the customer's self-initiated return call meaningfully reduce the legitimacy-establishment burden. A shorter intro is appropriate here. The absence of a codex-defined recycled-lead intro is the coaching gap, not Meg's execution.
coaching_implication: Flag to Kenny: the codex has no recycled-lead intro variant. This call is a candidate for building one. The design-framing move at 00:22 is the functional equivalent — consider formalizing it as the recycled-lead opener template.

quote: Oh, 05:30 is better. Because I work nights, so I'll probably be asleep most of the day.
stewart_read: The customer discloses a scheduling constraint (night-shift worker, asleep during the day) that makes the 05:30 slot non-negotiable and the appointment time-sensitive. This is a cooperation signal — he's explaining himself — but it also means any reschedule attempt will face real friction. Meg acknowledges it ('Makes sense. Okay. Gotcha.') but does not anchor the appointment with the button-up elements the codex requires: no computer anchor, no 'not driving' check, no verbal handshake.
alternative_hypothesis: The brevity of the call and the recycled-lead context may justify a compressed button-up. The customer is cooperative and the time is confirmed.
coaching_implication: The night-shift disclosure was the natural insertion point for the computer anchor and verbal handshake. Manager should replay 01:05–01:20 with the rep and ask: 'Where would you have put the computer check and the is-that-fair?' The customer's cooperative state at this moment made it the lowest-friction window in the call.

stewart_read: Meg asks 'you'll be home still, and we're not waking you up or anything?' — a reasonable check — and the customer confirms. This was the natural insertion point for the computer anchor ('And you'll be able to sit down at your computer to review the design?') and the verbal handshake ('Is that fair?'). Neither was inserted. The customer's 'K. No.' closes the window. The codex marks both elements as required and notes that skipping the computer anchor raises no-show rate.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have judged the call too short and the customer too cooperative to need the full button-up sequence. On a recycled lead with a self-initiated callback, that judgment has some merit.
coaching_implication: This is a mechanical gap, not a judgment gap. The rep had the right moment and didn't use it. Manager should drill the button-up sequence as a habit, not a situational choice. Suggested insertion point: immediately after 'we're not waking you up or anything?' — 'And you'll be able to sit down at your computer to review the design? Is that fair?'

quote: Make sure that when when we meet with you that you have your recent copy of your electric bill. We just need to get some details from that. But as long as you have it ready for the appointment, we should be good.
stewart_read: Deferring bill collection to the appointment is defensible on a recycled lead where the bill may already be on file and the customer's prior-contact uncertainty makes live collection awkward. The framing is functional but flat — 'we just need to get some details from that' does not anchor any bill pain or create curiosity. The codex requires bill capture AND emotional flip (alignment-against-utility); neither happens here. The deferred-collection path is the right call; the framing of why the bill matters is the missed opportunity.
alternative_hypothesis: On a 2-minute recycled callback, attempting a full bill-pain anchor would have been scope creep and likely would have broken the cooperative state the rep had built.
coaching_implication: The deferred-collection decision is correct. The coaching point is the framing: 'we just need to get some details' undersells the bill's role. Suggested alternative (not Ion-canonical — confirm phrasing with Kenny): 'Bring your most recent bill — the design is built around your actual usage, so having it there makes the numbers real for you.' This plants curiosity without extending the call.

quote: Just to let you know, we'll give you a call, couple hours beforehand, and we'll text you just to make sure that time still works.
stewart_read: The confirmation-call promise is a positive trust signal — it gives the customer a low-friction exit if the time stops working, which paradoxically increases show rate by reducing the anxiety of being locked in. However, this is the only button-up element Meg delivers. The codex requires: datetime confirmed (partial — done), one-hour block stated (missing), phone appointment specified (missing), no-driving/computer anchor (missing), is-that-fair handshake (missing), text-followup promise (partial — mentioned but not personalized per text_after_call requirements).
alternative_hypothesis: On a 2-minute recycled callback, a full button-up sequence may have felt disproportionate and risked re-opening resistance.
coaching_implication: The confirmation-call promise is good instinct. Manager should use this as the bridge: 'You did the confirmation call right — now let's add the computer anchor and the handshake right before that line. It's two sentences.' The goal is to make the full button-up feel like a natural extension of what the rep already does, not a new checklist.

quote: Last time we spoke with you, did we end up getting a copy of your electric bill?
stewart_read: Meg does not know whether the bill is already on file — she asks the customer, who also doesn't know ('my mom must have been there'). On a recycled lead, the codex flags that Ion may already have the bill from a prior touch, and repeating bill collection is redundant time-burn. The rep's uncertainty here is a CRM/pre-call prep gap, not a technique gap. She handles the uncertainty correctly by deferring to the appointment rather than forcing a live collection attempt.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have checked and genuinely found no bill on file — the question could be a confirmation check rather than a knowledge gap.
coaching_implication: Flag to manager: does the rep have access to prior-contact bill status before the call? If yes, this is a prep discipline gap. If no, this is a tooling gap that belongs in the Kenny/ops conversation about recycled-lead metadata. Either way, the rep should not be asking the customer to reconstruct prior-contact history.
